summary: Gruber argues that the AT&T data breach exposing millions of customers' call and text records demonstrates why RCS is a fundamentally flawed protocol that Apple shouldn't be adopting in iOS 18. He contends that any new messaging standard should require end-to-end encryption, which RCS lacks at the protocol level. Google's proprietary E2EE implementation only works between Google Messages users, and the RCS spec itself offers no encryption. Gruber acknowledges the pragmatic argument that carrier-based messaging is universal and should be improved, but counters that platforms like WhatsApp already serve as encrypted universal baselines in most countries. He suggests the effort spent pushing Apple toward RCS would have been better directed at getting Apple to release iMessage for Android.
key_points: RCS lacks end-to-end encryption at the protocol level; Google's E2EE is proprietary and only works between Google Messages users
New messaging platforms should require E2EE from the start — retrofitting encryption onto an already-deployed unencrypted protocol is nearly impossible
Apple's primary motivation for adopting RCS appears to be a Chinese government mandate, and authoritarian governments benefit from unencrypted messaging
Carrier-based messaging ties users to active SIM cards and specific devices, unlike modern platforms like iMessage, Signal, and WhatsApp
The real missed opportunity is Apple refusing to ship iMessage for Android — WhatsApp, not RCS, should fill the cross-platform baseline role
money_quotes: The only new messaging platforms that should gain any traction are those that not only support E2EE, but that require it.

Even if you trust law enforcement agencies to handle this sensitive data securely, it's clear, from this latest data breach alone, that the carriers themselves cannot be trusted. They're inept. They always have been inept. And my money says they always will be.
RCS should have been exclusively E2EE; instead, the standard offers no encryption at all.

Carrier-based messaging was best left as a legacy protocol. SMS wasn't dying, but it was slowly fading away, and should have been left for things like automated 'your table is ready' notifications from restaurants.
RCS is just going to give carrier-based messaging new legs that it shouldn't have gotten.
All of the effort spent pushing Apple to support RCS would have been better spent pushing Apple to ship iMessage for Android.
WhatsApp is free, secure, and works equally well on all phones.
key_insight: Adding RCS support to iOS legitimizes an unencrypted messaging protocol at a time when the industry should be moving exclusively toward end-to-end encryption, and the real solution to cross-platform messaging was always shipping iMessage for Android.
